subheading: Animate any still image into video with the leading models in one place: LTX-2.3, Wan, Veo, Kling, Seedance, and Grok.
extendedDescription:
The hardest part of any AI image to video tool is not the clicking, it is picking the right model. One engine nails cinematic camera moves, another holds a face steady, another is cheap enough to iterate on. Most tools hide that choice and lock you to a single model. These ComfyUI workflows put the leading models side by side, so you can match the model to the shot instead of forcing every shot through one.

The lineup reflects what is actually strong right now. LTX-2.3 is a fast, capable all-rounder and even handles image plus audio to video. Wan 2.5 and 2.6 are reliable and economical for everyday motion. Veo (from Google) leans cinematic. Kling 3.0 adds real motion control and longer takes. Seedance 2.0 is the popular pick for reference-driven and character-consistent clips, and Grok covers reference to video. Each is a real workflow you open and run, not a button that hides what it did.

The job is the same whichever you choose: you animate a photo by uploading a still, describing the motion or camera move you want, and letting the model predict how the scene should move while keeping the original frame consistent. This is true photo to video, not a slideshow. The model reads depth, subjects, and lighting in your image, then generates each frame so the result holds together rather than warping. A good motion prompt is short and concrete, naming what moves and how the camera travels.

Because the workflows are open, you control clip length where the model allows it, swap engines without redoing your setup, and re-run until the motion looks right. Treating one image to video generator as a fixed answer is the usual mistake; the better approach is to turn image into video with two models and keep the stronger take.

Everything runs in the browser on Comfy Cloud with no install, or locally in ComfyUI when you have the GPU for it. There are no watermarks on the workflows, so you can animate a product shot for an ad, make a short video from photo for social, bring a portrait or piece of art to life, or generate B-roll for a storyboard, then compare two models on the same image before you commit to the final render.

howToUse: Pick an image-to-video workflow and the model that fits your shot.
Open it on Comfy Cloud with no install, or load it in local ComfyUI.
Upload the still image you want to animate.
Write a short prompt describing the motion or camera movement.
Run the workflow, then try a different model on the same image to compare.
Download the clip you like at full quality.

faqItems.question: How do I turn a photo into a video with AI?
faqItems.answer: Upload your still image to one of the image-to-video workflows below and add a short prompt describing the motion or camera move. The model then animates the image into a short clip while keeping the original frame consistent.

faqItems.question: What is the best AI model for image to video?
faqItems.answer: It depends on the shot. Veo leans cinematic, Kling 3.0 offers motion control and longer takes, Wan 2.5 and 2.6 are economical all-rounders, LTX-2.3 is a fast generalist, and Seedance 2.0 is strong for reference-driven, character-consistent clips. You can try each one here.

faqItems.question: Can I run these AI image to video workflows myself?
faqItems.answer: Yes. These are open, editable ComfyUI workflows you can run locally, or on Comfy Cloud with no setup. You are not locked into a single model on the workflows themselves, and you can swap engines whenever you like.

faqItems.question: Can I add motion or camera movement to a still image?
faqItems.answer: Yes. A motion prompt guides how the subject and camera move, and several workflows add explicit controls. Kling motion control, for example, lets you direct movement rather than leaving it to chance.

faqItems.question: How long can the generated videos be?
faqItems.answer: Clip length depends on the model. Some produce a few seconds while others, like Kling, support longer durations. Pick the workflow whose model matches the length you need.

faqItems.question: Can I keep a character consistent across the clip?
faqItems.answer: Yes, that is what the Seedance 2.0 reference-to-video workflow is built for. It uses your image as a reference so the subject stays recognizable through the motion.

faqItems.question: Why does my generated video look warped or morphing?
faqItems.answer: Warping usually comes from asking for too much motion or from a busy image the model struggles to track. Start with a clear subject and a simple motion prompt, lower the motion strength if the workflow exposes it, and try a steadier model like Wan before reaching for a more aggressive one. A clean, well-lit input image is the single biggest fix.

faqItems.question: Can I use the videos commercially?
faqItems.answer: The workflows are open and editable, but commercial rights depend on each model's own license and terms. Some models permit commercial use and others restrict it, so check the usage terms for the specific model you choose before publishing.

faqItems.question: Do I need to install anything to run these workflows?
faqItems.answer: No. On Comfy Cloud the workflows open in your browser with nothing to install, so you can animate a still without setting up Python, CUDA, or model weights. If you prefer to run locally, you install ComfyUI on your own machine and use your own GPU instead.

faqItems.question: What hardware do I need to run these locally in ComfyUI?
faqItems.answer: It depends on the model: a distilled LTX-2.3 or the 5B Wan variant runs on a 12 to 16GB consumer GPU, while the larger 14B Wan and 13B LTX models want 24GB or more for comfortable frame counts. The API-based engines like Kling 3.0, Veo, and Seedance 2.0 run on Comfy Cloud rather than your own card, so you can reach them without heavy local hardware.

faqItems.question: Does this cost anything to run?
faqItems.answer: Comfy Cloud is a paid plan with tiered seats and starts at $20 per seat per month for the Standard tier, which suits browser-based generation without local setup. Running the same open workflows locally in ComfyUI uses your own GPU with no per-clip charge, so the trade-off is convenience versus owning the hardware.
